3

Adithya Patabendige, J.

The Petitioner, Sri Lankan Airlines Limited, is a duly incorporated company in Sri Lanka and

the national carrier engaged in commercial air transport operations. The Petitioner employs

pilots under written contracts of employment.

This application challenges the determination of the 2nd Respondent, the Assistant

Commissioner of Labour, relating to salary reductions implemented by the Petitioner in 2020

due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

Factual Background

Prior to the pandemic, the pilots employed by the Petitioner were remunerated under

contracts that provided for salaries denominated in United States dollars. Such salaries were

payable at the prevailing exchange rate.

With the global spread of COVID-19 in early 2020, international air travel declined

drastically, resulting in severe financial losses for airlines worldwide, including the Petitioner.

In response, the Petitioner introduced salary reductions for employees. From April 2020,

salaries payable in foreign currency were converted using a fixed exchange rate of 188.78

rupees per US dollar.

The 5th Respondent, Sri Lankan Airline Pilots’ Guild, lodged a complaint with the 1st

Respondent, the Commissioner of Labour, dated 02nd March 2021, marked P1, alleging that

these measures were imposed unilaterally and resulted in unlawful deductions from wages.

Acting on that complaint, the 3rd Respondent initiated proceedings and called for

explanations from the Petitioner.

After conducting an inquiry, the 3rd Respondent concluded that the reduction constituted

unauthorized deductions from wages and suggested to recover under the Wages Boards

Ordinance marked 1R11. Acting upon the findings contained in 1R11, the Assistant

Commissioner of Labour, the 2nd Respondent, issued the Order marked P5, directing the

Petitioner to pay the deducted wages of Rs. 1,928,063,281.56.

Aggrieved by the said determination, the Petitioner has invoked the jurisdiction of this court

seeking to quash the impugned decision.

4

Reliefs Sought

The Petitioner seeks, inter alia,

• A mandate in the nature of a writ of certiorari to quash the determination of the 2nd

Respondent, marked P5.

• A writ of prohibition preventing the enforcement of the said decision,

• Other incidental reliefs.

Position of the 1st to 3rd Respondents

The 1st to 3rd Respondents contend that the inquiry was conducted lawfully pursuant to a

complaint of deducting the wages. They assert that the evidence disclosed that the Petitioner

had unilaterally reduced salaries otherwise payable under the contract of employment.

It is their position that the Petitioner failed to produce adequate documentary justification for

the deductions, whereas the Respondents produced a detailed computation of the amounts

due.

The 3rd Respondent further maintains that the inquiry was conducted after affording the

Petitioner an opportunity to present its position, and a determination was issued after

considering material submitted by both parties. Accordingly, they deny any violation of

Natural Justice.

Position of the 4th and 5th Respondents

The 4th and 5th Respondents have filed formal objections opposing the application. Their

position substantially supports the determination made by the 3rd Respondent.

They aver that the salary reductions were imposed unilaterally without the consent of

employees, especially the pilots, and without statutory authority. Particular reliance is placed

on the contractual terms governing remuneration and the salary records produced during the

inquiry proceedings.

5

They further contend that the Petitioner altered the method of salary computation, including

adopting a fixed exchange rate for USD-based remuneration, thereby reducing the salaries

payable to the pilots.

In addition, the 4th and 5th Respondents raised a preliminary objection that the Petitioner has

failed to disclose material facts relating to the inquiry proceedings and the documentary

evidence considered by the 3rd Respondent.

Issues for Determination

The principal issues that arise for determination are,

• Whether the 3rd Respondent acted within the jurisdiction,

• Whether the applicable statute was correctly applied.

• Whether the principles of natural justice were violated.

• Whether the impugned determination is illegal, irrational, or otherwise liable to be

quashed in the exercise of writ jurisdiction.

Alleged Suppression of Material Facts

A principal objection raised by the Respondents is that the Petitioner has not made a full and

frank disclosure of all material facts.

It is contended that the petitioner omits or inadequately discloses,

• The full contents of the complaint lodged by the pilot’s guilt.

• The communications initiating the inquiry.

• The salary data and computations submitted during the Proceedings.

• The extent and method of salary reduction adopted by the Petitioner.

• The participation of the Petitioner in the inquiry process.

It is well established that the Petitioner invoking writ jurisdiction must approach the court

with utmost candor. However, not every omission constitutes suppression. The Court must

6

determine whether the omitted facts are material and whether their non-disclosure would

have affected the exercise of discretion.

Having considered the pleadings and documents, I am of the view that although certain

aspects of the inquiry proceedings are not fully set out in the Petition, the essential nature of

the dispute has been disclosed. Therefore, the alleged nondisclosure does not by itself warrant

dismissal of the application. The Court must be satisfied that any alleged nondisclosure was

both material and deliberate and that it would have affected the exercise of the Court's

discretion.

Jurisdiction and Applicable Law

The Petitioner contends that the proceedings were initiated under the Industrial Disputes Act,

but the determination was made under the Wages Boards Ordinance. Thereby, rendering the

decision without jurisdiction.

Inquiry Initiation under the Industrial Disputes Act

The Petitioner further contends that the proceedings were initiated under the Industrial

Disputes Act and that a determination under the Wages Boards Ordinance is therefore invalid.

I am unable to accept this contention.

The complaint throughout concerned salary reductions implemented by the Petitioner and the

resulting short payment of wages. The Petitioner was aware of the nature of the allegations,

participated in the inquiry, submitted explanations, and produced documentary material

relating to the salary structure and the measures adopted.

In these circumstances, the Petitioner cannot be said to have been misled as to the subject

matter of the proceedings or deprived of an opportunity to present its case and thereby

violates natural justice.

The validity of administrative action must be determined by the substance of the matter rather

than the terminology used in preliminary communications. Even if reference was initially

made to the Industrial Disputes Act, such reference does not invalidate the subsequent

7

decision under the statutory provisions governing payment of wages where the facts disclose

a matter falling within that framework.

In order to examine this contention, it is necessary to consider the scheme of the Wages

Boards Ordinance.

Structure of the Ordinance

The Ordinance is divided into two parts,

• Part I — Provisions relating to all trades.

• Part II — Provisions relating to particular trades.

Part I contains general provisions applicable to workers employed in any trade, whereas Part

II applies only to specific trades for which Wages Boards have been established and decisions

made.

The present dispute does not concern minimum wages or conditions of employment fixed by

a Wages Board under Part II. It concerns alleged reduction and short payment of wages

otherwise payable under contracts of employment. Therefore, the applicability of Part I must

be examined.

Section 2 - Payment of Wages

Section 2(a) of the Ordinance provides that,

The employer of workers in any trade shall comply with the following provisions of this

section regarding the payment of wages to every such worker: -

(a) He shall, subject as hereinafter provided, pay such wages in legal tender directly to the

worker, without any deduction other than an authorized deduction, as hereinafter defined,

made with the consent of the worker:

Provided that the aggregate of the deductions so made at any one time shall not exceed,

(i) seventy-five per centum of the wages due, in the case of a worker in any trade specified

by the Minister for the purposes of this paragraph by Notification published in the Gazette;

and

8

(ii) fifty per centum of the wages due, in the case of a worker employed in any other trade;

Provided, further, that nothing in the preceding provisions of this paragraph shall affect or

be deemed to affect-

(i) any deduction authorized to be made from such wages by the Income Tax Ordinance*

or any other written law; or

(ii) any retention or payment of the whole or any part of such wages made in pursuance of

or compliance with any order, process or decree made or issued by any court of law.

For the purposes of this paragraph a payment which, immediately after the wages are paid

to the worker, is made out of the wages by the worker to the employer or to an agent of the

employer, shall be deemed to be a deduction from the wages.

In this paragraph "authorized deduction" means a deduction made in such manner and

subject to such conditions, if any, as may be prescribed in respect of-

(i) any advance of money made by the employer to the worker,

(ii) any payment which, at the instance of the worker, is made out of the wages of the

worker by the employer to any person other than the employer or an agent of the employer

in order to discharge any obligation of the worker or for any other purpose, or

(iii) any other prescribed matter.

The section sets limits on deductions and recognizes only those authorized by law or made

with the worker's consent.

This provision applies to employers of workers “in any trade.” It is not confined to trades

governed by a Wages Board decision.

In the present case, the complaint before the labour authorities was that the Petitioner

unilaterally reduced salaries otherwise payable under the contracts of employment by

adopting a fixed exchange rate and other measures. Such an allegation directly concerns

deductions from wages and therefore falls within the scope of Section 2.

9

Section 3 - Wage Records

Section 3 requires employers to maintain clear and accurate wage records specifying, inter

alia:

• the wage period,

• the workers paid,

• the wages paid,

• the date of payment, and

• deductions made.

The purpose of this provision is to enable verification of whether wages due have been

properly paid.

Section 3D — Recovery of Short Payment

Section 3D empowers the Commissioner of Labour, where proper wage records are not

maintained or produced, to assess the wages or short payment of wages on the basis of

available evidence and to recover such sums through the statutory mechanism.

This provision is directly relevant to complaints alleging short payment or unlawful

deductions.

The complaint in the present case was precisely that the salaries contractually payable were

reduced. Therefore, the proceedings conducted by the labour authorities fall squarely within

the recovery framework provided by Section 3D.

Interpretation Section (Section 64)

Section 64 defines several key terms.

The term “trade” is defined broadly to include:

“any industry, business, undertaking, occupation, profession or calling…”

An airline company engaged in commercial air transport clearly falls within the ordinary

meaning of an “industry” or “business.”

The term “wages” includes:

10

“any remuneration due in respect of overtime work or of any holiday.”

It indicates that remuneration payable for employment is within the scope of the Ordinance.

The term “worker” is defined as: (within the meaning of employer)

“any person employed to perform any work in any trade.”

Pilots employed by the Petitioner to perform duties in its airline operations fall within this

definition.

Accordingly, the definitions contained in Section 64 are sufficiently wide to encompass the

employment relationship in question.

Section 65 - Effect of the Ordinance

Section 65 provides that, subject to express provisions to the contrary, the Ordinance shall

have effect notwithstanding anything contained in any other written law, and in case of

conflict, its provisions shall prevail.

While this section does not itself create jurisdiction, it reflects the legislative intention that

the provisions relating to payment of wages operate with overriding force where applicable.

Having regard to the structure of the Ordinance and the nature of the complaint, I am of the

view that,

• The provisions of Part I of the Wages Boards Ordinance are applicable to all trades,

including the employment in question.

• The complaint of unilateral salary reduction and short payment falls within Sections

2, 3, and 3D.

• The definitions contained in Section 64 encompass the Petitioner’s undertaking and

the employees concerned.

• The Petitioner was aware of the nature of the inquiry and suffered no prejudice.

• The 2nd Respondent did not act without jurisdiction in issuing the determination

marked P5 based on the findings contained in document 1R11.

11

Whether the Principles of Natural Justice were Violated

The Petitioner contends that the inquiry conducted by the 3rd Respondent was procedurally

unfair and that the determination was issued in violation of the principles of natural justice.

The material placed before the Court, especially the document dated 23rd March 2021,

marked 1R2, discloses that upon receipt of the complaint lodged by the 5th Respondent, the

3rd Respondent informed the Petitioner of the date of the inquiry and sent a copy of the

complaint of the 5th Respondent. Both parties participated in the inquiry dated 09th April

2021, as reflected in the proceedings of that date, marked 1R3.

The 3rd Respondent, by his letter dated 01st November 2021, marked 1R5, informed the

Petitioner to submit all the documents relevant to the complaint made by the 5th Respondent.

The document dated 15th November 2021, marked 1R6, indicates that both parties had

participated on that date.

The Petitioner and the 5th Respondent filed their written submissions on 22nd November

2021, marked 1R7 and 06th of May 2021, marked 1R4, respectively.

The inquiry report marked 1R11 reflects that the 3rd Respondent considered the positions of

both parties, examined the salary data, and made findings regarding the unilateral reduction

of wages.

There is no material to suggest that the Petitioner was denied notice of the allegations, an

opportunity to be heard, or the opportunity to place relevant material before the inquiry

officer. Nor is there evidence that any relevant submissions made by the Petitioner were

disregarded without consideration.

The Petitioner has made a general allegation that the 3rd Respondent acted in breach of the

principles of natural justice. However, the Petition does not specify which component of

natural justice was allegedly violated or how the alleged violation occurred.

A party alleging a breach must demonstrate, with particularity, how these principles have

been infringed and how such infringement has caused prejudice.

A bare assertion of violation of natural justice, unsupported by specific facts, is insufficient to

invoke the supervisory jurisdiction of this Court. Judicial review is concerned with

demonstrable procedural unfairness, not speculative or generalized allegations.

12

Natural justice does not require a formal trial-type hearing unless expressly provided by

statute. What is required is a fair opportunity to present a case.

In the present case, the Petitioner actively participated in the proceedings and cannot now

contend that the fair hearing was not given.

Accordingly, I find no violation of the principles of natural justice.

Reasons of the 3rd Respondent

The Petitioner further contends that the impugned determination is arbitrary and unsupported

by adequate reasons.

The order marked P5, issued by the 2nd Respondent, is based on the inquiry findings

contained in document 1R11. The inquiry report sets out

• the nature of the complaint.

• the contractual basis of remuneration.

• the method of salary reduction adopted.

• the evidence submitted by both parties.

• the conclusion that the reduction constituted unauthorized deductions.

Attention is drawn to the contents, especially paragraphs 1 to 6 of the 1R11, which state as

follows.

01. ඉහත කාරණය සම්බන්ධයයන් ශ්‍රී ලංකා ගුවන් සමාගයම් යසේවය කරන ගුවන් කපිතාන්

වරුන්යේ 2020/04 මස සිට යම් දක්වා ඔවුන්යේ එකඟතාවයයන් යතාරව ආයතනය

ඔවුන්යේ වැටුයෙන් වැටුප් අඩුකර යගවීම පිළිබඳව කරන ලද ෙැමිණිල්ල හා බැ යඳේ.

02. යමහිදී ආයතනය යසේවක එකඟතාවයයන් යතාර ගුවන් කපිතාන්වරුන්යේ වැටුප් අඩුකර

යගවන අතර තවද ඔවුන්යේ ෙත්වවීම් ලිපියේ යකාන්යේසි අනුව ඔවුන්ට වැටුප් යගවීම

ය ාලර්වලින් සිදුවන අතර රයේ ෙවතින ය ාලරයට සායප්ක්ෂව රුපියල් අගය හා වැටුප්

යගවීමට එකඟ වී ඇති නමුත්ව 2020/04 මස සිට ය ාලරයට සායප්ක්ෂව රුපියල යවනසේවීම

හා වැටුප් යනායගවන අතර ආයතනය විසින් 2020/04 මස සිට නිශ්ේිත අගයන් වන

රු.188.78 අගය මත ගණනය කර එම යසේවකයන්ට වැටුප් යගයේ.

13

03. ඒ අනුව යදොර්ශ්වය අතර ෙවතින ෙත්වවීම් ලිපියේ යකාන්යේසි ක කිරීමක් වන අතර

ආයතනය එේකොර්ිකව තීරණය කර එම අගය මත ගණනය කර වැටුප් යගවීම නීතයානුකූල

යනායේ.

04. ව/උ ොර්ශ්වය එම කරුණු පිළිගන්නා අතර නමුත්ව යමයතක් යදොර්ශ්වය අතර ඒ සඳහා

එකඟතාවයක් යනාමැත.

05. ව/උ ොර්ශ්වය විසින් යසේවක වැටුප් අඩු කිරීම පිළිබඳව විසේතර කාර්යාලය යවත ඉදිරිෙත්ව

යනාකරන ලද අතර ෙැමිණිළි ොර්ශ්වය විසින් අදාල යල්ඛන ඉදිරිෙත්ව කර ඇත.

නිර්යේශ්ය

01 ආයතනය විසින් යසේවක එකඟතාවයකින ේ යතාරව යසේවක වැටුප් අඩුකිරීමක් කර යගවීම්කර

ඇති අතර එම හිඟ වැටුෙ ෙැමිණිළි ොර්ශ්වය විසින් ලබා දුන් යතාරතුරු යල්ඛන අනුව

ගණනය කර ඇත (අමුණා ඇත).

02 ඒ අනුව එම හිඟ මුදල් අයකර ගැනීම සඳහා නියේදන යැවීම සුදුසු ය.

A careful examination of paragraphs 1 to 7 of the inquiry report marked 1R11 reveals that the

3rd Respondent has identified the essential factual and legal components of the dispute and

recorded findings thereon. The report notes that the complaint concerned the reduction of

pilots’ salaries from April 2020 without their consent, notwithstanding that the contracts of

employment provided for remuneration in United States dollars, calculated at the prevailing

exchange rate. It further observes that, instead of paying salaries in accordance with those

contractual terms, the Petitioner had adopted a fixed exchange rate of Rs. 188.78 per US

dollar from April 2020, resulting in reduced payments. The 3rd Respondent characterizes this

conduct as a unilateral departure from the contractual basis of remuneration and therefore not

legally justified. Significantly, the report records that, while the Petitioner acknowledged the

reduction in salaries, no agreement between the parties had been reached authorizing such a

variation. The 3rd Respondent also notes that the Petitioner failed to submit detailed

particulars of the deductions, whereas the complainant party furnished documentary material

enabling computation of the alleged shortfall. On that basis, the inquiry concludes that

unauthorized deductions had occurred and recommends that steps be taken to recover the

arrears calculated from the information supplied by the complainant.

Accordingly, the report cannot be regarded as devoid of reasons. It discloses the complaint

considered, the contractual framework, the factual findings regarding unilateral reduction, the

14

evidentiary basis relied upon, and the rationale for the recommended course of action. The

correctness or merits of the conclusion reached by the 3rd Respondent cannot be examined in

proceedings for judicial review.

Administrative decisions are not required to contain elaborate judicial reasoning. It is

sufficient if the decision discloses the basis upon which it was made.

The House of Lords, in the case of South Bucks District Council and another v Porter

(FC) [2004] UKHL 33, considered this issue, and Lord Brown stated that the reasons for a

decision must be intelligible, and they must be adequate. They must enable the reader to

understand why the matter was decided as it was and what conclusions were reached on the

"principal important controversial issues", disclosing how any issue of law or fact was

resolved. Reasons can be briefly stated, the degree of particularity required depending

entirely on the nature of the issues falling for decision. The reasoning must not give rise to a

substantial doubt as to whether the decision-maker erred in law, for example by

misunderstanding some relevant policy or some other important matter or by failing to reach

a rational decision on relevant grounds. But such adverse inference will not readily be

drawn. The reasons need refer only to the main issues in the dispute, not to every material

consideration.

The reasons in the inquiry report (1R11), read together with the formal order (P5), adequately

disclose the factual and legal basis for the determination.

Therefore, the impugned decision cannot be characterized as arbitrary or unreasonable.

Impact of Covid -19 and Salary Reduction

A central argument advanced by the Petitioner is that salary reductions were implemented

across the global aviation industry during the COVID-19 pandemic and that the measures

adopted were necessary for survival.

There is no doubt that the pandemic caused unprecedented disruption to international air

transport and severe financial hardship to airline operators worldwide. Many airlines

implemented temporary cost-cutting measures, including reductions in remuneration.

15

However, the legality of such measures in Sri Lanka must be determined in accordance with

applicable domestic law and the contractual rights of employees.

The fact that similar measures were adopted in other countries or by other airlines does not,

by itself, justify unilateral reductions as lawful under Sri Lankan law.

Where contracts of employment specify the remuneration payable, any alteration of those

terms ordinarily requires consent or statutory authority unless the contract itself permits

variation.

Accordingly, the global practice relied upon by the Petitioner cannot, by itself, override the

contractual rights of employees or the statutory protections governing the payment of wages

under the Wages Boards Ordinance.

In this regard, the decision of the House of Lords in Rigby v Ferodo Ltd [1988] ICR 29 is

instructive. In that case, the employer, faced with financial difficulties, imposed a wage

reduction without the consent of the employee. The employee continued to work under

protest and later claimed the difference between the contractual wage and the reduced amount

paid. The employer contended that the employee had accepted the new terms by continuing in

employment or that the reduction terminated the original contract.

Rejecting those arguments, Lord Oliver of Aylmerton held that it is common ground that the

unilateral imposition by an employer of a reduction in the agreed remuneration of an

employee constitutes a fundamental and repudiatory breach of the contract of employment

which, if accepted by the employee, would terminate the contract forthwith.

The decision further establishes that even where an employee continues to work, such

conduct does not necessarily amount to acceptance of the new terms, particularly where the

reduction is disputed or accepted under protest.

Accordingly, while the economic consequences of the COVID-19 pandemic are undeniable,

such circumstances cannot, without more, displace contractual obligations or statutory

protections governing the payment of wages. The critical inquiry remains whether the

reduction was effected in a manner authorized by law or by agreement of the parties.

16

Suggestion of Reference for Industrial Arbitration by the 3rd Respondent

The Petitioner states that during the inquiry proceedings, the 3rd Respondent suggested that

the dispute be referred for industrial arbitration, but that the 1st and 2nd Respondents failed to

take steps in that regard (vide. P3).

Under Section 3 of the Industrial Disputes Act, the power to refer a dispute for arbitration lies

with the Minister and is exercised at his discretion.

The failure to make such a reference does not invalidate proceedings otherwise lawfully

conducted under another statute, nor does it confer a right upon a party to insist upon

arbitration.

The 1st to 3rd Respondents have taken the position that, since the Petitioner admitted

implementing the salary reductions, the dispute concerned the recovery of short-paid wages

rather than an unresolved industrial dispute warranting arbitration.

In any event, the absence of a reference for arbitration does not affect the validity of the

determination made under the Wages Boards Ordinance.

Rationality and Legality of the Decision

In an application for judicial review, the intervention of the Court is warranted only where the

decision is

• illegal or made without jurisdiction.

• irrational or unreasonable.

• procedurally unfair.

In the present case,

• The inquiry was conducted by a Labour Officer, the 3rd Respondent.

• The subject matter concerned alleged unlawful deductions from wages.

• The Petitioner participated in the proceedings.

• Reasons for the determination are available.

17

• No procedural unfairness is established.

The Court cannot interfere merely because another view may be possible on the merits.

Conclusion

Having considered all the material placed before the Court, I hold that,

• The 3rd Respondent, Labour Officer, acted within his jurisdiction.

• The applicable statutory provisions were correctly invoked.

• The Petitioner was afforded a fair opportunity to present its case.

• The determination is supported by reasons.

• No ground has been established for the exercise of writ jurisdiction.

Accordingly, the application must fail.

Order

For the foregoing reasons, I find no basis to interfere with the impugned determination.

Accordingly, the application for Writs of Certiorari and Prohibition is refused and dismissed.

In the circumstances of the case, I make no order as to costs.

JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL

Dhammika Ganepola, J

I agree.

JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL

JUDGEMENT

B. Sasi Mahendran, J.

The 1st, 2nd,4th, and 5th Accused Appellants (herein after referred to as the 1st, 2nd,

4th, and 5th Appellants), along with the 3rd Accused, Nijabdeen Mohemed Rimsan,

were indicted before the High Court of Galle on the following counts,

1. On or about 2.12.2008, committed an offence which is punishable under

Section 140 of the Penal Code by being members of an unlawful assembly

with the common purpose of causing injury to Stephen Jeyawandran alias

Sinnappu.

2. Causing the death of Stephen Jeyachandran alias Sinnappu, being

members of an unlawful assembly, and committed the offence of murder

punishable under Section 296 read with Section 146 of the Penal Code.

3. Causing the death of the said Stephen Jeyachandran alias Sinnappu

which is punishable under Section 296 read with the section 32 of the

Penal Code.

At the trial, the prosecution presented evidence through five witnesses and

marking productions P1-P9 and thereafter closed its case. All the Accused, in their

defence, made dock statements.

Upon conclusion of the trial, the Learned Judge of the High Court delivered

judgment on 7th May 2019. The 1st, 2nd, 4th, and 5th appellants were found guilty

on the third count of the indictment and sentenced to death, and were acquitted

of the first and second counts. The 3rd accused was acquitted and discharged from

all three charges.

Being dissatisfied with both the conviction and the sentence imposed by the

Learned High Court Judge, the Appellants preferred an appeal before this Court,

articulating the following grounds in support of their challenge.

1. Evidence of the sole eyewitness namely sandamali (PW/3), is fraught with

serious infirmities which renders the conviction wholly unsafe.

Page 4 of 12

2. Assessment of the testimonial trustworthiness of the sole eyewitness by

the trial court is deficient and flawed.

3. Trial court has flawed on the principles relating to Sec.27 recoveries

consequently causing serious prejudice to the accused appellants.

The facts and circumstances of this case are as follows,

PW 3, R.M.P.Rathnawali, the sole eyewitness of the case, who is a resident in the

same area, Status Road, where the deceased and the accused were residing. She

states that she had known the appellants and the deceased for over 15 years and

that the deceased was a friend of her brother. On 02.12.2008, at around 11.00 p.m.,

the deceased visited the residence of the witness and requested a sum of Rs. 200/-, but

shortly thereafter, upon the arrival of his wife, he departed. Later that night, PW 3 stated

that she went to a boutique to obtain a reload, where she again encountered the deceased,

who was standing across a bridge and requested money once more. In order to hand over

the money, the witness accompanied the deceased back towards her residence.

On their way she saw the 4th appellant hit the deceased's head from behind with

a hockey stick. After the blow on his head, the deceased had fallen on the ground

screaming අම්මෝ, and the 1st appellant came and attacked the deceased on his

neck with a Manna Knife. After the first appellants’s attack, the witness stated

that the 2nd and 5th , appellants, along with the 3rd, Accused, arrived carrying two

poles and a Manna knife. She testified that the 2nd and 5th Appellants assaulted

the deceased with the poles. The witness observed the incident under the presence of

light, and PW 7, who subsequently visited the scene, confirmed that there was indeed

illumination at the location.

The witness stated that when she attempted to intervene, the 4th Appellant

shouted at her to stop. PW 1 further claimed that after she rushed to the

Grandpass Police to report the incident. In her testimony, PW3 added that the

police did not record any statement on that day; instead, her statement was taken

after 14 days, as she had been hiding due to the Appellants searching for her. She

further stated that the accused attacked and caused damage to her house.

Page 5 of 12

Page 116 of the brief,

ප්‍ර : මම කිව්වොත් තමන් කට උත්තරය දීලො ති්ෙන්්න් දවස් 14 කට පස්්ස කියලො තමන්

පිළි ගන්නවොද ?

උ : ඒකත් ්වන්න පුළුවන්. ඇයි කියන්්න් ්ේ ්ගොල්ලො මොවත් ් ොයො ්ගන හිටියො. මමත්

දන්නවො කියලො. ඊට පහු්වනිදො මම කිව්ව.

During the cross-examination, the counsel for the appellants suggested that she

had been engaged in an affair with the deceased during the relevant period and

therefore she is fabricating the story. The witness categorically denied this

suggestion. The defence suggested that the witness had not gone to the police

station to make a complaint. In response, the witness stated that she did, in fact,

go to the police station, and that was the reason the police subsequently came. This

assertion was disputed by PW 6, who stated that the witness had not come police to make

a complaint.

She categorically stated that she had seen the 4th appellant, striking the deceased

on the head with a hockey stick, followed immediately by the 1st appellant, who

stabbed the deceased. It was suggested by the counsel for the appellants that the

witness was fabricating a story, as it was not possible for neighbors not to have

heard of the attack, as it was a highly residential area. However, the witness

categorically denies such allegations, stating that there were no houses closer to

the place of the incident.

It is pertinent to note that an omission was marked by the counsel for the 4th

Appellant stating that the witness in her statement had not mentioned the hockey

stick. I note that instead the witness has referred to it as a “polla” and the learned

trial Judge considered this point and held that it does not affect her credibility.

PW 6, IP Vajira S K Dhanapala, stated that on 03.12.2008, the deceased's mother

arrived at the police station and informed them that her son was stabbed to death.

The witness stated that, following the arrest of the appellant, from their statements he

recovered a Manna knife from the 1st Appellant, a knife from the 2nd Appellant, and

Page 6 of 12

another Manna knife from the 3rd accused. The 1st and 2nd Appellants did not dispute

these recoveries. However, the 3rd accused denied that a knife was recovered from his

possession, asserting instead that it was introduced by the police.

PW 11, Dr. A. Dayapala, JMO, testified that he conducted the post-mortem

examination on 03.12.2008. He testified that the deceased sustained a total of fifteen

injuries, comprising thirteen cut wounds and two stab wounds. PW 11 further explained

that injury No. 3 was fatal, which was a cut in the head, as the cut penetrated

deep into the brain and could have easily caused death. He opined that it

constituted a very great antecedent probability of death. He also noted the

possibility that the injuries may have been inflicted by weapons marked as P2

(Manna Knife), P8 (knife), and P5 (knife). Additionally, he testified that it was not

possible for a single individual to cause such fatal injuries and that it was highly

likely they were inflicted by more than one person.

Furthermore, the Judicial Medical Officer testified that, of the two stab injuries, the second was located in the middle of the chest and had penetrated deeply, reaching the principal blood vessel of the left lung. He noted that the absence of significant external bleeding was unusual. The JMO further opined that this stab injury was inflicted very close to, or subsequent to, the death caused by head injury (Injury No. 03).

ස අ්නක් ප ත්ත ්මොට ආකොරයට. එම ඇනුේ තුවොලය පපුව ඇතුලට කිදො ෙ ලො තිබු්ේ

න ස්වොමිණි. මස් ්ගොෙයට පමණක් සීමො ්වලො තිබුණො. තව ඇනුේ තුවොලයක් දකින්නට

ල බුණො ්සන්ටිමීටර් ්දකක පමණ ඇනුේ තුවොලයක්. තිරස්ව පිහිටි පපු්ව ම ද ්කොට්ස්

දක්නට ල බු්ේ. තනපුඩු ති්ෙන ්ර්ඛෝව. එහි එය ඉතො ග ඹුරට ්ගොස් වේ ්පන ල්ල

ප්‍රධොන ්ල න ර ප්‍ර්ේශය දක්වො ගමන් කර තිබුණො.

Page 7 of 12

Upon conclusion of the prosecution's case, all the appellants gave dock statements

and denied the allegation of committing the murder of the deceased. None of the

appellants denied the recovery of the production, but the third accused denied this

allegation.

The Learned High Court Judge observed the opinion formed by the JMO injuries

sustained by the deceased were inflicted by more than one individual which

corroborates the testimony of PW 03 regarding the manner in which the injuries

were inflicted by the Appellants. I note that the Learned High Court Judge has

correctly evaluated the evidence of PW 3, which was corroborated by the doctor’s

testimony, PW 11.

The principal grievance advanced by the defence was that the evidence of PW3

should be rejected on the ground that her statement to the police was made after

a delay of 14 days. In addressing this contention, it is necessary to consider

whether the testimony of a witness who comes forward belatedly can be accepted.

Our courts have held that while delay may raise concerns regarding credibility, it

does not automatically render the testimony inadmissible. The Court must

evaluate whether the delay has been satisfactorily explained and whether the

explanation is justifiable with the circumstances of the case.

At this juncture, reference must be made to judicial decisions that have examined

the admissibility and reliability of delayed statements.

In Sumanasena Vs Attorney General [1999] 3 SLR 137 at page 140, His Lordship

Justice Jayasuriya held as follows.

"just because the witness is a belated witness the Court ought not to reject

his testimony on that score alone and that a Court must inquire into the

reason for the delay and if the reason for the delay is plausible and

justifiable the Court could act on the evidence of a belated witness.”

Page 8 of 12

In Ajith Samarakoon Vs the Republic [2004] 2 SLR 209 at page 220, His Lordship

Justice Jayasuriya held as follows.

“Just because the statement of a witness is belated the Court is not entitled

to reject such testimony. In applying the Test of Spontaneity the Test of

Contemporaneity and the Test of Promptness the Court ought to

scrupulously proceed to examine the reasons for the delay. If the reasons

for the delay adduced by the witness are justifiable and probable the trial

Judge is entitled to act on the evidence of a witness who had made a belated

statement.”

In the case of Dhannasiri vs. The Republic of Sri Lanka 2012 (1) SLR 268

Tilakawardane, J held inter alia;

"Two critical tests before considering belated evidence as reliable " evidence

are: firstly reasons for delay and secondly, whether those reasons are

justifiable."

Further in the case of Dharmasiri v Republic of Sri Lanka 2010 (2) SLR 241, Sisira

de Abrew, J held inter alia;

"Because the witness is a belated witness, Court ought not to reject his

testimony on that score alone. Court must inquire into the reason for the

delay and if the reason for the delay is plausible and justifiable, the Court

could act on the evidence of the belated witness."

Further, in the case of Udagam Vs. Attorney General 2000 (2) SLR 103 relied by

the learned Counsel for the Appellant it was held that;

“Evidence is infirm, unsafe and unreliable to act upon considering the

following;

(i) the belated statement made to the Police with delay not explained with

acceptable reasons.”

Page 9 of 12

According to the above judicial literature, when assessing the evidentiary value of

the belated statement made by PW3 to the police, this Court must first consider

the reasons for the delay, and secondly, whether such reasons are legally and

factually justifiable. The testimony of the witness demonstrates that the delay in

PW3’ s statement was occasioned by fear for her personal safety. Learned Counsel

for the Appellant argued that PW3 had made her statement to the police only after

a lapse of 14 days from the incident. While the delay is undeniable, the explanation

provided that PW3 refrained from immediately implicating the Appellant due to

apprehension for her own security appears credible.

It is pertinent to refer to the evidence of PW 3.

පිළි ගන්නවොද ?

උ : ඒකත් ්වන්න පුළුවන්. ඇයි කියන්්න් ්ම ්ගොල්ලො මොවත් ් ොයො ්ගන හිටියො. මමත්

ැංගිලො තමයි හිටි්ේ. ඊට පස්්ස ්පොලිසිය ද න්ීමක් දීලො තමයි මම ආ්ව. කට උත්තරයක්

කියලො කියන්්න් කවුද ම රු්ව දන්නවො ද කියන එක විතරයි ඇහු්ව. මම කිවවො ඔව මම

දන්නවො කියලො. ඊට පහු්වනිදො මම කිව්ව.

He further opined that injury No. 03 was inflicted very close to, or immediately after, the fatal head injury (Injury No 3).

්ව

On the perusal of the judgment of the learned Trial Judge, it is apparent that

adequate consideration has been given to the contradictions and omissions

highlighted by the defence, and the finding was rightly made that they do not go

to the root of the evidence of PW 3. Although PW 3 did not make a spontaneous

statement to the police, there is no reason to reject her testimony. An analysis of

her evidence leads to the conclusion that she has not fabricated the story, as her

version is corroborated by the medical evidence, which is independent and reliable.

Also, I note that the learned High Court Judge has correctly disregarded the

appellant's version and has given the reasons for that. Perusal of the dock

statements of the appellants it reveale a brief denial of involvement. There is no

specific plea of evidence, such as alibi, which has been raised by any of the

appellant. PW 3 has stated how these accused were involved with the murder. In

the light of the cogent incrimination evidence adduced by the prosecution, there is

no attempt to explain.

For the above said reasons, I hold that there was sufficient material for the learned

trial judge to come to the finding that all four accused were acting in furtherance

of a common murderous intention to murder Stephen Jeyawandran alias

Sinnappu.

I am also mindful that the appellants did not dispute the fact that they attacked

PW 3’s house, and that they absconded from the police for 14 days without offering

any explanation. I hold that the prosecution has proved the case beyond

Page 12 of 12

reasonable doubt, the simultaneous participation of all four accused. The learned

High Court Judge has correctly identified the role of each accused separately and

concluded that they all shared a common intention to commit the offence.

Considering these factors, I am of the view that none of the grounds of appeal

presented by the appellant's counsel warrant interference with the findings of the

Learned High Court Judge. The judgment dated 7 May 2019 is upheld, and the

conviction, along with the sentence, remains in effect.

The appeal is dismissed.

JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL

Amal Ranaraja, J.

I AGREE

text:
Page 1 of 6

IN THE COURT OF APPEAL OF THE DEMOCRATIC SOCIALIST REPUBLIC

OF SRI LANKA

In the matter of an application under and in terms of Section 331 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act, No. 15 of 1979. Hon. Attorney General, Attorney General’s Department, Colombo 12.

Complainant CA HCC 216/23 Vs.

High Court of Colombo Case No. HC 342/17 Bodaram Baravegedara Wickremesinghe Mudiyanselage Nalindu Nihal Samarakoon alias Nalindu Wickramasinghe Accused

AND NOW BETWEEN Bodaram Baravegedara Wickremesinghe Mudiyanselage Nalindu Nihal Samarakoon alias Nalindu Wickramasinghe Accused-Appellants Vs, The Hon. Attorney General, Attorney General’s Department, Colombo 12.

Complainant-Respondent Before: B. Sasi Mahendran. J, Amal Ranaraja. J,

Page 2 of 6

Counsel: Sarath Jayamanne, P.C., with Vineshka Mendis, Prashan Wickramarathne, A. Wickramarachchi, Himalshi Fernando and Uvindu Bandara for the Accused-Appellant

Malik Azeez, SC for the Respondent

Argued on: 10.02.2026 Judgment on: 23.03.2026

Judgment Amal Ranaraja. J,

1. The accused-appellant (herein after referred to as the appellant) has

been indicted in the High Court of Colombo in High Court case no 342/2017. The charge in the indictment is as follows;

i. Between the dates of 01st of August, 2009 and 31st of August, 2009, within the jurisdiction of this Court, in Kalaniya, you have conducted an incestuous relationship prohibited under Section 364A (1) of the Penal Code as amended by Penal Code (Amendment) Act No.22 of 1995 by committing the offence of rape on the daughter of your younger sister, namely, Kendagolla Gedara Shahini Nilanthika, who was a person under the age of 16. Thereby you have committed an offence punishable under Section 364(3) of the Penal Code as amended by Penal Code (Amendment) Act No.22 of 1995. 2. At the conclusion of the trial, the learned High Court judge has

convicted the appellant of the charge and sentenced him as follows,

i. 15 years rigorous imprisonment and also imposed a fine of Rs.25,000/- with a term of 1 year rigorous imprisonment, in default. The appellant has also been directed to pay a sum of Rs.250,000/- as compensation to PW01 with a term of two years rigorous imprisonment, in default. 3. The appellant aggrieved by the conviction, the disputed judgment and

the sentencing order has preferred the instant appeal to this Court.

Case of the Prosecution

4. PW01 is the appellant’s niece, specifically the daughter of his maternal

half-sister. This means PW01’s mother and the appellant share a common biological mother but have different fathers.

Page 3 of 6

5. At the time of the alleged incidents in August 2009, PW01 has resided

in Pundaluoya while the appellant has lived in Kiribathgoda. During the school holidays that month, it is alleged that the appellant with the permission of PW01’s parents has brought PW01 to his residence. The stated purpose for this visit had been for the appellant to provide PW01 with English language instruction. PW01’s parents have reportedly agreed to this arrangement without objection largely because, the appellant’s wife was a teacher, lending an air of legitimacy to the educational pretext. 6. It is further alleged that on two separate occasions during PW01’s stay,

when the appellant’s wife was absent from the house, the appellant had engaged in sexual intercourse with PW01. 7. The formal complaint regarding these alleged incidents have been

lodged with the investigating authorities on September 28th, 2010, approximately over a year after the alleged incidents described in the indictment. 8. Following the receipt of the complaint, an investigation has

commenced. PW01 has undergone a medical examination conducted by PW04, i.e. Dr. H.T.D.W. Ariyaratne. The resulting medico-legal report has been tendered as evidence and marked පැ 05. Case of the Appellant

9. The appellant has maintained a firm denial of any sexual involvement

with PW01. His version of events is that he intervened in a family matter at the behest of his sister, who is PW01’s mother. Concerned about PW01’s purported affair with a boy, his sister has asked the appellant to confront PW01 about the same. During this confrontation the appellant has acknowledged that he physically disciplined PW01 by slapping her. He has further contended that PW01’s father, angered by the appellant’s actions, has subsequently pressured or instructed PW01 to invent the sexual assault claim as an act of retaliation against the appellant. 10. The Grounds of appeal are as follows,

i. Did the learned High Court Judge err in law to properly evaluate the evidence of PW01?

ii. Did the learned High Court Judge err in law by using certain

items of evidence as corroboration?

iii. Did the learned High Court judge err in law by convicting the

appellant without rejecting the dock statement?

Page 4 of 6

11. The process of reporting a crime to the police can often be fraught with

emotional, psychological, and social challenges. Many individuals who experience wrongdoing may hesitate to make a complaint immediately, resulting in delays. Understanding the reasons behind such delays is crucial as well as acknowledging the potential consequences that can arise from them. 12. One of the most prominent reasons individuals delay reporting crimes

is the fear of retaliation from the perpetrator. This fear can be particularly acute if the offender is known to the victim or if the crime has instilled a sense of vulnerability in them. 13. Many victims especially in cases of sexual assault or abuse, may feel

an overwhelming sense of shame or embarrassment, which can prevent them from reporting the incident. Social stigma surrounding certain offences can lead to feelings of isolation or judgement, making victims reluctant to share their experiences with the authorities. 14. Trust plays a critical role in the decision to report a crime. A history of

negative experiences with law enforcement whether personal or observed can lead individuals to doubt that their complaints will be taken seriously or dealt with appropriately. 15. The emotional aftermath of experiencing a crime can be profound.

Victims may experience shock, confusion, or trauma which can cloud their judgement and decision-making abilities causing delays in their realisation and subsequent reporting. 16. Practical obstacles such as lack of transportation, language barriers

or difficulty in accessing information about how to file a complaint can also contribute to delays. 17. The Court having considered the foregoing has examined PW01’s

testimony to ascertain the reasons for the delay in lodging the first complaint. However, this Court found that PW01 has failed to provide a satisfactory explanation for this delay.

18. Courts often assess credibility by reference to the promptness of a

complaint. An unexplained delay can undermine the perceived reliability of the complainant’s narrative particularly where the case depends on oral testimony.

19. In Dr. Ariyaratne’s medico-legal report (පැ 05), he has stated that upon

examination of PW01’s external genitalia, he found no fresh or old injuries. He has further noted that the hymen was fimbriated a normal anatomical variant. 20. These findings do not corroborate PW01’s narrative. However, they

also do not definitively rule out the occurrence of sexual intercourse.

Page 5 of 6

Consequently, the medical evidence is inconclusive meaning it neither confirms nor disproves whether sexual intercourse took place. 21. It is an established principle of evidentiary law, that when a particular

piece of evidence is equally capable of supporting the prosecution’s case and the accused’s narrative, its interpretive ambiguity must be resolved in favour of the accused. Therefore, should Dr. Ariyaratne’s evidence present such vague findings it cannot legitimately be held to corroborate the allegations advanced against the appellant. 22. In Alim vs. Wijesinghe (S.I.Police, Batticaloa) 38 CLW 95, it has been

held,

“Where the same facts are capable of an inference in favour of the accused and also an inference against him, the inference consistent with the accused’s innocence should be preferred.” 23. Accordingly, it not being corroborated by medical evidence or other

credible circumstances, PW01’s narrative submitted after an unexplained delay lacks reliability and cannot be acted upon.

24. I shall now consider the principles relevant to the evaluation of a dock

statement. When evaluating dock statement, the following guidelines must be considered;

i. A dock statement should be considered as evidence subject to infirmities that it was not sworn testimony and not tested by cross-examination. ii. If the docks statement is believed, it must be acted upon. iii. If the dock statement creates a reasonable doubt in the prosecution’s case, the defence of the accused must succeed. iv. The dock statement of one accused person should not be considered against the other accused. 25. In The Queen v D. G. De S. Kularatne, 71 NLR 529, it has been held,

“....when an unsworn statement is made by the accused from the dock, the jurors must be informed that such statement must be looked upon as evidence, subject however to the infirmity that the accused had deliberately refrained from giving sworn testimony. But the jury must also be directed that (a) if they believe the unsworn statement it must be acted upon, (b) if it raises a reasonable doubt in their minds about the case for the prosecution, the defence must succeed, and (c) that it should not be used against another accused.”

Page 6 of 6

26. Although the learned High Court Judge has … the legal principles

governing the evaluation of the dock statement in the disputed judgement, he has failed to apply those principles when assessing the appellant’s own dock statement. Moreover, the disputed judgement does not reveal the learned High Court Judge’s definitive position on the issue.

27. As discussed above, the testimony of PW01 is not convincing and

unreliable. The offence stated in the charge, was allegedly committed approximately fifteen years ago. In those circumstances, I believe that this matter is not suitable for a retrial. 28. In the case of The Queen vs. G.K.Jayasinghe 69 NLR 314, Sansoni, J,

has stated,

“…we have considered whether we should order a new trial in this case. We do not take that course, because there has been a lapse of three years since the commission of the offences, and because of our own view of the unreliable nature of the accomplice’s evidence on which alone the prosecution case rests. We accordingly direct that the judgment of acquittal be entered.” 29. Accordingly, I am not inclined to order a retrial.

30. Accordingly, I set aside the conviction, disputed judgement, and

sentencing order and acquit the appellant of the charge. Appeal allowed I make no order regarding costs. 31. The Registrar of this Court is directed to communicate the judgment

to the High Court of Colombo for compliance.

Judge of the Court of Appeal B. Sasi Mahendran, J.
